texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Flue-gas cooling in post-combustion capture plants
texts.text: Flue-gas cooling in post-combustion capture plants

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Hanne M. Kvamsdal a , ∗ , Geir Haugen a , Hallvard F. Svendsen b
texts.text: Hanne M. Kvamsdal a , ∗ , Geir Haugen a , Hallvard F. Svendsen b

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: a Department of Process Technology, SINTEF Materials and Chemistry, Post Box 4760 Sluppen, N-7465 Trondheim, Norway

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: a b s t r a c t
texts.text: a b s t r a c t

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the present paper, the results of an activity performed in the ongoing EU project, CESAR (CO2 Enhanced Separation and Recovery) are presented. In the CESAR project five different baseline power-plants are considered. These consist of two ligniteand two bituminous-coal fired plants while the fifth is fuelled by natural gas. Part of the design work relates to identifying the benefits attainable through appropriate integration of flue-gas cooling to these baseline power-plants. It is shown that the approximate reductions in capture-cost for the studied (optimal) cases are of the order of 20% for the lignite-coal cases, 10% for the bituminous coal cases and 13% for the natural gas case using MEA as solvent. The reductions are basically due to the inclusion of pre-cooling. However, a positive effect of intercooling was also found for all the coal cases, while a negative cost effect was found for the natural gas case. For piperazine as solvent the positive effect of cooling was much more pronounced than for MEA and especially the effect of inter-cooling.
texts.text: In the present paper, the results of an activity performed in the ongoing EU project, CESAR (CO2 Enhanced Separation and Recovery) are presented. In the CESAR project five different baseline power-plants are considered. These consist of two ligniteand two bituminous-coal fired plants while the fifth is fuelled by natural gas. Part of the design work relates to identifying the benefits attainable through appropriate integration of flue-gas cooling to these baseline power-plants. It is shown that the approximate reductions in capture-cost for the studied (optimal) cases are of the order of 20% for the lignite-coal cases, 10% for the bituminous coal cases and 13% for the natural gas case using MEA as solvent. The reductions are basically due to the inclusion of pre-cooling. However, a positive effect of intercooling was also found for all the coal cases, while a negative cost effect was found for the natural gas case. For piperazine as solvent the positive effect of cooling was much more pronounced than for MEA and especially the effect of inter-cooling.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1. Introduction
texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In post-combustion capture, CO2 is recovered from flue gas from fossil fuelled power plants and the most appropriate separation technology is presently considered to be chemical absorption.
texts.text: In post-combustion capture, CO2 is recovered from flue gas from fossil fuelled power plants and the most appropriate separation technology is presently considered to be chemical absorption.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Amine absorption is yet the only commercial technology available for CO2 capture. However, the energy efficiency loss and the investment cost associated with such technology are still high. Therefore, it is crucial to identify and develop means for reduction of these parameters.
texts.text: Amine absorption is yet the only commercial technology available for CO2 capture. However, the energy efficiency loss and the investment cost associated with such technology are still high. Therefore, it is crucial to identify and develop means for reduction of these parameters.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the ongoing EU project 'CO2 Enhanced Separation and Recovery', with the acronym CESAR, the primary goal of the project is to reduce the cost of post-combustion CO2 capture to 15 D /tCO2 captured. These cost reductions are planned achieved through several different process improvements as well as proper selection of new solvent systems.
texts.text: In the ongoing EU project 'CO2 Enhanced Separation and Recovery', with the acronym CESAR, the primary goal of the project is to reduce the cost of post-combustion CO2 capture to 15 D /tCO2 captured. These cost reductions are planned achieved through several different process improvements as well as proper selection of new solvent systems.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the absorber column, operating in a counter-current manner where regenerated solvent is fed to the top of the column, meeting CO2 rich-flue gas fed to the absorber bottom, the solvent temperature increases as CO2 is absorbed through the bed. At the top of the column, the colder solvent will cause some water condensation and further down absorption
texts.text: In the absorber column, operating in a counter-current manner where regenerated solvent is fed to the top of the column, meeting CO2 rich-flue gas fed to the absorber bottom, the solvent temperature increases as CO2 is absorbed through the bed. At the top of the column, the colder solvent will cause some water condensation and further down absorption

texts.label: text
texts.orig: leads to higher temperatures. The resulting temperature profile along the column therefore normally shows a pronounced temperature bulge. The magnitude and location is dependent on several factors including solvent flow-rate and gas inlet CO2 content (Kvamsdal and Rochelle, 2008). Also, the resistance to mass transfer and kinetics may be of importance. The masstransfer resistance and kinetic resistances will decrease with increasing temperature while the equilibrium will become less favourable. Thus the temperature-bulge, if not appropriately controlled (to increase the pertinent thermodynamic driving forces), can contribute to an overall lowering of the absorber outlet rich loading. However, if the equilibrium controlled driving forces dominate, a worthwhile compromise can be found through heat removal; either by inter-cooling within or precooling prior to the absorber.
texts.text: leads to higher temperatures. The resulting temperature profile along the column therefore normally shows a pronounced temperature bulge. The magnitude and location is dependent on several factors including solvent flow-rate and gas inlet CO2 content (Kvamsdal and Rochelle, 2008). Also, the resistance to mass transfer and kinetics may be of importance. The masstransfer resistance and kinetic resistances will decrease with increasing temperature while the equilibrium will become less favourable. Thus the temperature-bulge, if not appropriately controlled (to increase the pertinent thermodynamic driving forces), can contribute to an overall lowering of the absorber outlet rich loading. However, if the equilibrium controlled driving forces dominate, a worthwhile compromise can be found through heat removal; either by inter-cooling within or precooling prior to the absorber.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The study has been conducted by means of both flowsheet simulations and a thorough cost-analysis. The solvent was primarily 30 wt% monoethanolamine (MEA), which is the industrial standard today. However, it was expected that a different solvent may imply different effects. Thus some of the cases, originally defined for the MEA solvent, were studied also for 30 wt% piperazine, which in general exhibits faster kinetics (Cullinane and Rochelle, 2006; Freeman et al., 2009; Hartono et al., 2009).
texts.text: The study has been conducted by means of both flowsheet simulations and a thorough cost-analysis. The solvent was primarily 30 wt% monoethanolamine (MEA), which is the industrial standard today. However, it was expected that a different solvent may imply different effects. Thus some of the cases, originally defined for the MEA solvent, were studied also for 30 wt% piperazine, which in general exhibits faster kinetics (Cullinane and Rochelle, 2006; Freeman et al., 2009; Hartono et al., 2009).

texts.orig: 2.2. Methodology and definition of case studies
texts.text: 2.2. Methodology and definition of case studies

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The design and operation of the absorption process are greatly influenced by: (a) the overall mass balance of the CO2 and (b) the energy balance over the desorber unit. These two aspects are expressed by the following formulas:
texts.text: The design and operation of the absorption process are greatly influenced by: (a) the overall mass balance of the CO2 and (b) the energy balance over the desorber unit. These two aspects are expressed by the following formulas:

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: R · y CO2 ˙ N g , a = m A · ( ˛ r -˛ l ) · ˙ V l (1)

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: Q r = -n CO2 · /Delta1H abs + ( ˙ N g,d -n CO2 ) · /Delta1Hvap + ˙ V l · /SUB l · Cp l ( T bottom,d -T top,d ) (2)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Here, R is the CO2 capture level, y CO2 is the molar fraction of CO2 in the absorber inlet flue gas, ˙ N g is the gas molar flow rate (index 'a' means absorber, index 'd' means desorber), m A is the molar concentration of amine in the solvent, ˛ r is the rich loading, ˛ l is the lean loading, ˙ V l is the solvent volumetric flow, Q r is the reboiler duty, n CO2 is the molar flow rate of CO2 desorbed in the desorber, /Delta1 Habs is the heat of absorption, ˙ N g,d is the molar flow rate of steam + CO2 leaving the stripper, /Delta1Hvap is the heat of water vaporization, /SUB l is the average liquid density, and Cp l is the average liquid heat capacity. This means that for a given capture rate it will be beneficial to have a large cyclic capacity, m A · ( ˛ r -˛ l ), so that the solvent flow-rate can be reduced. However, this effect becomes less important if the temperature approach (of the cross heat exchanger) is small. The latter means reduced energy requirement in the reboiler (Eq. (2)). A large cyclic capacity can be obtained by either a high rich loading, a low lean loading, or a combination. A high rich loading means that the concentration of CO2 in the liquid in the bottom of the absorber column is approaching the equilibrium concentration.
texts.text: Here, R is the CO2 capture level, y CO2 is the molar fraction of CO2 in the absorber inlet flue gas, ˙ N g is the gas molar flow rate (index 'a' means absorber, index 'd' means desorber), m A is the molar concentration of amine in the solvent, ˛ r is the rich loading, ˛ l is the lean loading, ˙ V l is the solvent volumetric flow, Q r is the reboiler duty, n CO2 is the molar flow rate of CO2 desorbed in the desorber, /Delta1 Habs is the heat of absorption, ˙ N g,d is the molar flow rate of steam + CO2 leaving the stripper, /Delta1Hvap is the heat of water vaporization, /SUB l is the average liquid density, and Cp l is the average liquid heat capacity. This means that for a given capture rate it will be beneficial to have a large cyclic capacity, m A · ( ˛ r -˛ l ), so that the solvent flow-rate can be reduced. However, this effect becomes less important if the temperature approach (of the cross heat exchanger) is small. The latter means reduced energy requirement in the reboiler (Eq. (2)). A large cyclic capacity can be obtained by either a high rich loading, a low lean loading, or a combination. A high rich loading means that the concentration of CO2 in the liquid in the bottom of the absorber column is approaching the equilibrium concentration.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: All simulations were executed in ProTreat (Optimized Gas Treating, Inc. v3.14) while the relevant cost calculations were done using an in-house tool (ECON2). It should be noted that the absolute accuracy of the latter tool may not be the best as it is based on open information. However, for comparative studies and benchmarking of relative similar chemical processes it should yield reasonably accurate results.
texts.text: All simulations were executed in ProTreat (Optimized Gas Treating, Inc. v3.14) while the relevant cost calculations were done using an in-house tool (ECON2). It should be noted that the absolute accuracy of the latter tool may not be the best as it is based on open information. However, for comparative studies and benchmarking of relative similar chemical processes it should yield reasonably accurate results.

texts.orig: 3. Simulation

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.1. MEA
texts.text: 3.1. MEA

texts.label: text
texts.orig: All the sub-cases listed in the previous section for all the baseline power-plant cases were simulated in ProTreat. The reboiler-duty or thermal heat requirement in the reboiler is the largest contributor to the total energy requirement (60-70% of total) in the capture plant. Together with the liquid flow-rate and the rich loading (lean loading is set equal for all sub-cases as the amount of captured CO2 is the same). The important performance parameters are given in Table 5 for all the cases.
texts.text: All the sub-cases listed in the previous section for all the baseline power-plant cases were simulated in ProTreat. The reboiler-duty or thermal heat requirement in the reboiler is the largest contributor to the total energy requirement (60-70% of total) in the capture plant. Together with the liquid flow-rate and the rich loading (lean loading is set equal for all sub-cases as the amount of captured CO2 is the same). The important performance parameters are given in Table 5 for all the cases.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Table 5 shows that cooling of the flue-gas has a pronounced effect on the reboiler-duty for all the baseline power-plant cases, but the reduction decreases with increasing cooling effort. This can be partly explained by the higher rich loading (as mentioned previously the equilibrium will be more favourable at lower temperatures) and thus lower solvent flowrate. The decreasing liquid-to-gas ratio on weight basis, will strengthen the temperature bulge as shown in Kvamsdal et al. (2010) and thus the effect of increased cooling.
texts.text: Table 5 shows that cooling of the flue-gas has a pronounced effect on the reboiler-duty for all the baseline power-plant cases, but the reduction decreases with increasing cooling effort. This can be partly explained by the higher rich loading (as mentioned previously the equilibrium will be more favourable at lower temperatures) and thus lower solvent flowrate. The decreasing liquid-to-gas ratio on weight basis, will strengthen the temperature bulge as shown in Kvamsdal et al. (2010) and thus the effect of increased cooling.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For all the coal cases, there is a clear combined effect of inter-cooling and pre-cooling, but the effect of inter-cooling alone is higher than that of pre-cooling for both the bituminous coal cases and the lignite coal cases. However, the effect of flue gas pre-cooling is strong. The bituminous coal cases are already cooled to 50 ◦ C and since the pre-cooling to 50 ◦ C
texts.text: For all the coal cases, there is a clear combined effect of inter-cooling and pre-cooling, but the effect of inter-cooling alone is higher than that of pre-cooling for both the bituminous coal cases and the lignite coal cases. However, the effect of flue gas pre-cooling is strong. The bituminous coal cases are already cooled to 50 ◦ C and since the pre-cooling to 50 ◦ C

texts.label: footnote
texts.orig: a The LP steam price is determined from the electricity price assuming that one unit of steam withdrawn from the powerplant corresponds to 0.2858 kWh loss of electric energy (based on Bolland and Undrum, 2003).
texts.text: a The LP steam price is determined from the electricity price assuming that one unit of steam withdrawn from the powerplant corresponds to 0.2858 kWh loss of electric energy (based on Bolland and Undrum, 2003).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: shows higher effect in general for the other cases than further reduction in pre-cooling temperature, this might explain why inter-cooling seems better than pre-cooling for the bituminous coal cases. There is hardly any effect of the combined intercooling and pre-cooling for the NG case and pre-cooling alone is clearly a better approach than inter-cooling alone. Since the temperature bulge is much lower for the NG case than for the coal cases and since the inter-cooling seems to have more effect on the bulge than pre-cooling this may explain this lack of effect. However, there is an effect of maximum 13% by applying pre-cooling and this is explained by the positive influence of the low temperature on the equilibrium in the bottom of the column.
texts.text: shows higher effect in general for the other cases than further reduction in pre-cooling temperature, this might explain why inter-cooling seems better than pre-cooling for the bituminous coal cases. There is hardly any effect of the combined intercooling and pre-cooling for the NG case and pre-cooling alone is clearly a better approach than inter-cooling alone. Since the temperature bulge is much lower for the NG case than for the coal cases and since the inter-cooling seems to have more effect on the bulge than pre-cooling this may explain this lack of effect. However, there is an effect of maximum 13% by applying pre-cooling and this is explained by the positive influence of the low temperature on the equilibrium in the bottom of the column.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Both inter-cooling and pre-cooling will have a positive effect on the mass transfer in the bottom of the absorber because of the increased driving forces. This can be seen from the increased rich loading (see Table 5, in which the rich loading, liquid flow-rate, and rich temperature are given for the MEA cases). Since the rich loading is increased, the liquid flowrate can be decreased (for the same capture level and lean loading) and the reboiler-duty is thereby decreased. Vaporization of water may also have an important cooling effect on the liquid in the bottom. If the flue gas fed to the absorber is unsaturated, as in both the base case and the pre-cooling to 50 ◦ C, water will start to evaporate immediately. If also the liquid temperature is higher than the flue gas temperature in case of saturated flue gas (pre-cooling to 40 ◦ C and 30 ◦ C), the gas temperature will immediately increase and vaporization will take place.
texts.text: Both inter-cooling and pre-cooling will have a positive effect on the mass transfer in the bottom of the absorber because of the increased driving forces. This can be seen from the increased rich loading (see Table 5, in which the rich loading, liquid flow-rate, and rich temperature are given for the MEA cases). Since the rich loading is increased, the liquid flowrate can be decreased (for the same capture level and lean loading) and the reboiler-duty is thereby decreased. Vaporization of water may also have an important cooling effect on the liquid in the bottom. If the flue gas fed to the absorber is unsaturated, as in both the base case and the pre-cooling to 50 ◦ C, water will start to evaporate immediately. If also the liquid temperature is higher than the flue gas temperature in case of saturated flue gas (pre-cooling to 40 ◦ C and 30 ◦ C), the gas temperature will immediately increase and vaporization will take place.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Inter-cooling alone, when using MEA as solvent, has only a small influence on the rich amine outlet temperature compared to all the pre-cooling cases. It seems that the liquid regains its high temperature towards the bottom of the column due to the warmer inlet gas. It should be noted, however, that the location of the inter-cooling is not optimized in this study and maybe the effect would have been larger closer to the bottom (there is a mass-transfer pinch near the bottom), see Karimi et al. (2010). In the combined cases (for the German lignite coal) it can be seen (Table 5) that the rich stream temperatures are lower than the flue gas temperatures, while they are higher than the flue gas temperatures for the cases with pre-cooling only. This observation is not valid for the natural gas cases and the reason for the smaller combined effect than in all coal cases can be the differences in the liquid to gas (L/G) ratio. L/G increases with increasing CO2 content in the flue gas (coal cases). Thus the cooling effect by water vaporization is less in the coal cases than for the natural gas cases. Since there is also more CO2 absorption per m 3 solvent, the temperature in the absorber column is generally higher for the coal cases. Since also the effect of water vaporization is higher for the natural gas case there will be a weaker effect of inter-cooling, but a stronger effect of pre-cooling even though cooling the flue gas below a dew point temperature (40 ◦ C and lower) is more energy intensive than cooling of a liquid.
texts.text: Inter-cooling alone, when using MEA as solvent, has only a small influence on the rich amine outlet temperature compared to all the pre-cooling cases. It seems that the liquid regains its high temperature towards the bottom of the column due to the warmer inlet gas. It should be noted, however, that the location of the inter-cooling is not optimized in this study and maybe the effect would have been larger closer to the bottom (there is a mass-transfer pinch near the bottom), see Karimi et al. (2010). In the combined cases (for the German lignite coal) it can be seen (Table 5) that the rich stream temperatures are lower than the flue gas temperatures, while they are higher than the flue gas temperatures for the cases with pre-cooling only. This observation is not valid for the natural gas cases and the reason for the smaller combined effect than in all coal cases can be the differences in the liquid to gas (L/G) ratio. L/G increases with increasing CO2 content in the flue gas (coal cases). Thus the cooling effect by water vaporization is less in the coal cases than for the natural gas cases. Since there is also more CO2 absorption per m 3 solvent, the temperature in the absorber column is generally higher for the coal cases. Since also the effect of water vaporization is higher for the natural gas case there will be a weaker effect of inter-cooling, but a stronger effect of pre-cooling even though cooling the flue gas below a dew point temperature (40 ◦ C and lower) is more energy intensive than cooling of a liquid.

texts.text: 3.2. Piperazine

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Only the German lignite cases were simulated in ProTreat using a 30 wt% piperazine solution. Inter-cooling to 30 ◦ C as in the MEA cases, was also done for the piperazine solution. However, there can be a problem with precipitation at lower temperatures, but this has not been taken into account in the simulations. In Fig. 3 the specific reboiler duty for each case is shown for the various cases. It should be noted that the same

texts.text: In Figs. 3 and 4 are shown the effects of inter and precooling on the piperazine and MEA systems, respectively. Comparing the cases without inter-cooling it is clear that the effect of pre-cooling is much stronger for piperazine than for

texts.label: text
texts.orig: MEA. The explanation for this is complex, but probably two main contributions are the equilibrium temperature sensitivity and the indirect effect on mass transfer. Piperazine has a somewhat higher equilibrium temperature sensitivity than MEA. This will improve the equilibrium position at the bottom of the tower slightly more for piperazine than for MEA. The strongest effect is however caused by the faster kinetics of piperazine. This leads to a generally closer approach to equilibrium at the tower bottom for piperazine than for MEA. In
texts.text: MEA. The explanation for this is complex, but probably two main contributions are the equilibrium temperature sensitivity and the indirect effect on mass transfer. Piperazine has a somewhat higher equilibrium temperature sensitivity than MEA. This will improve the equilibrium position at the bottom of the tower slightly more for piperazine than for MEA. The strongest effect is however caused by the faster kinetics of piperazine. This leads to a generally closer approach to equilibrium at the tower bottom for piperazine than for MEA. In

texts.label: text
texts.orig: other words, the chances for a pinch are higher for piperazine than for MEA. A shift in the equilibrium will therefore have a significantly stronger effect for piperazine than for MEA. Also the combined effect of inter and pre-cooling is stronger for piperazine for the same reasons. It is also clear that the extra effect of inter-cooling is stronger for piperazine. We believe that the explanations are to be found mainly in the reasons given above. The piperazine system, being the fastest, will be more dominated by the equilibrium than MEA, and can thus benefit from the double cooling to a larger extent than can MEA.

We believe that the explanations are to be found mainly in the reasons given above. The piperazine system, being the fastest, will be more dominated by the equilibrium than MEA, and can thus benefit from the double cooling to a larger extent than can MEA.

texts.orig: 4.1. MEA
texts.text: 4.1. MEA

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The CO2 capture costs given in euro per ton of captured CO2 for each of the sub-cases of pre-cooling and without and with pre-cooling are shown in Figs. 5 and 6 for the two lignite powerplant cases, respectively.
texts.text: The CO2 capture costs given in euro per ton of captured CO2 for each of the sub-cases of pre-cooling and without and with pre-cooling are shown in Figs. 5 and 6 for the two lignite powerplant cases, respectively.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As for the reboiler duty, there is a similar tendency with respect to the combined effect of inter-cooling and pre-cooling on the capture cost for the lignite coal cases (Figs. 5 and 6).
texts.text: As for the reboiler duty, there is a similar tendency with respect to the combined effect of inter-cooling and pre-cooling on the capture cost for the lignite coal cases (Figs. 5 and 6).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: However, the effect of inter-cooling alone seems drastically reduced, while the effect of only pre-cooling to 50 ◦ C is slightly increased. The reason for this is that the total cooling water demand (not shown here) remains similar as in the base-case for pre-cooling to 50 ◦ C, while it is increased for the intercooling case. In the former case, the increased cooling-water demand in the pre-cooler is counteracted by less cooling in the lean solvent cooler and the stripper condenser due to improved solvent cyclic capacity (reduced solvent and more favourable equilibrium (higher rich loading)), which reduce stripping steam in the stripper overhead condenser, respectively.
texts.text: However, the effect of inter-cooling alone seems drastically reduced, while the effect of only pre-cooling to 50 ◦ C is slightly increased. The reason for this is that the total cooling water demand (not shown here) remains similar as in the base-case for pre-cooling to 50 ◦ C, while it is increased for the intercooling case. In the former case, the increased cooling-water demand in the pre-cooler is counteracted by less cooling in the lean solvent cooler and the stripper condenser due to improved solvent cyclic capacity (reduced solvent and more favourable equilibrium (higher rich loading)), which reduce stripping steam in the stripper overhead condenser, respectively.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the pre-cooling cases, the electricity demand in the blower will be reduced as the inlet temperature of the blower is lower and thereby the utility costs are reduced. As the reboiler duty is decreasing with increasing pre-cooling, there seems to be a minimum capture cost in the case of precooling, at around 40 ◦ C for both the lignite coal cases. The very strong decrease from the case without pre-cooling (base case) to pre-cooling to 50 ◦ C, can, as for the German lignite case, be explained by the dominating effect of reduced cost by reduced reboiler duty (which influences both CAPEX and OPEX). With more pre-cooling the effect of increased

The very strong decrease from the case without pre-cooling (base case) to pre-cooling to 50 ◦ C, can, as for the German lignite case, be explained by the dominating effect of reduced cost by reduced reboiler duty (which influences both CAPEX and OPEX). With more pre-cooling the effect of increased

As stated, the combined effect of pre-cooling and inter-cooling is not that pronounced (the reduction in capture cost at pre-cooling to 40 ◦ C is 19% for the combined case, while the case with only pre-cooling is 17% compared to base case) and it is not strongly recommended. In addition to increased CAPEX the side-stream from the absorber will definitely also influence the complexity of the plant.

texts.label: text

However, as for the reboiler duty for these two cases, the effect is not that pronounced between the base cases and the pre-cooling to 40 ◦ C as these cases are already cooled to 50 ◦ C in the pre-treatment plant.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Regarding the natural gas case (see Fig. 9), the effect of inter-cooling is actually negative meaning that the increased CAPEX is not counteracted by the slight improvement in the reboiler duty. However, it is interesting to note that reducing the pre-cooling temperature even further may have a positive effect, but then other means of cooling by sea-water is required, which will influence negatively on both CAPEX and OPEX.

However, it is interesting to note that reducing the pre-cooling temperature even further may have a positive effect, but then other means of cooling by sea-water is required, which will influence negatively on both CAPEX and OPEX.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.2. Piperazine
texts.text: 4.2. Piperazine

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The CO2 capture costs for the piperazine cases are shown in Fig. 10. The total cost effect is larger for pre-cooling than for inter-cooling even though the opposite trend is found for the reboiler-duty (Fig. 3) and even though the CAPEX of the intercooler is less than for the pre-cooler. The reason is mainly that the total cooling-water demand is slightly increased in the case of inter-cooling alone, while it is decreased with only precooling. As for MEA, the pre-cooling cases imply less cooling water requirement in the lean cooler and the condenser and the pre-cooling cases will require less electricity in the blower, which reduces the utility cost. Compared to the MEA based lignite case, the cost of using piperazine is somewhat lower mainly due to less CAPEX for the rich/lean heat-exchanger and the reboiler. The latter is due lower reboiler duty (13% lower in the case of pre-cooling to 30 ◦ C), which also influence on the operating cost. There is also a slightly lower cooling

The reason is mainly that the total cooling-water demand is slightly increased in the case of inter-cooling alone, while it is decreased with only precooling. As for MEA, the pre-cooling cases imply less cooling water requirement in the lean cooler and the condenser and the pre-cooling cases will require less electricity in the blower, which reduces the utility cost. Compared to the MEA based lignite case, the cost of using piperazine is somewhat lower mainly due to less CAPEX for the rich/lean heat-exchanger and the reboiler. texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 5. Summary and conclusions
texts.text: 5. Summary and conclusions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The effects of flue-gas pre-cooling and absorber inter-cooling for 5 different baseline power-plants with post-combustion capture have been studied. The base solvent was MEA, while the effect of using piperazine as solvent was conducted only for the German lignite power plant. Different cases of precooling, inter-cooling and combined cooling were defined and the main conclusions using MEA as solvent are as follows:
texts.text: The effects of flue-gas pre-cooling and absorber inter-cooling for 5 different baseline power-plants with post-combustion capture have been studied. The base solvent was MEA, while the effect of using piperazine as solvent was conducted only for the German lignite power plant. Different cases of precooling, inter-cooling and combined cooling were defined and the main conclusions using MEA as solvent are as follows:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · All cases exhibit a positive energy effect of employing precooling.
texts.text: All cases exhibit a positive energy effect of employing precooling.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · The main cases for which the flue gas temperature is higher than 50 ◦ C exhibits the best reduction in both reboiler-duty and capture cost by employing pre-cooling. This concerns the two lignite coal cases (Cases 1 and 2) and the natural gas case (Case 5).
texts.text: The main cases for which the flue gas temperature is higher than 50 ◦ C exhibits the best reduction in both reboiler-duty and capture cost by employing pre-cooling. This concerns the two lignite coal cases (Cases 1 and 2) and the natural gas case (Case 5).

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Combined pre-cooling and inter-cooling has a positive effect on the reboiler duty and the capture cost for all lignite coal and bituminous coal cases. However, while the lowest precooling temperature (30 ◦ C) implies the lowest reboiler-duty, the optimal pre-cooling temperature seems to be closer to 40 ◦ C regarding the capture cost for these cases.
texts.text: Combined pre-cooling and inter-cooling has a positive effect on the reboiler duty and the capture cost for all lignite coal and bituminous coal cases. However, while the lowest precooling temperature (30 ◦ C) implies the lowest reboiler-duty, the optimal pre-cooling temperature seems to be closer to 40 ◦ C regarding the capture cost for these cases.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · For the natural gas case (Case 5) inter-cooling has only a slight positive effect regarding reboiler-duty, while it actually has a negative effect regarding the capture cost.
texts.text: For the natural gas case (Case 5) inter-cooling has only a slight positive effect regarding reboiler-duty, while it actually has a negative effect regarding the capture cost.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Pre-cooling has a very positive effect in the natural gas case and it seems that there is a potential in reducing the absorber inlet temperature even further than 30 ◦ C, which was the lowest pre-cooling temperature studied here.
texts.text: Pre-cooling has a very positive effect in the natural gas case and it seems that there is a potential in reducing the absorber inlet temperature even further than 30 ◦ C, which was the lowest pre-cooling temperature studied here.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The main conclusions for the piperazine cases are as follows:
texts.text: The main conclusions for the piperazine cases are as follows:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · There is a positive effect of employing both pre-cooling and inter-cooling.
texts.text: There is a positive effect of employing both pre-cooling and inter-cooling.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · There is a larger effect of employing inter-cooling than precooling on the reboiler-duty, while there is an opposite trend regarding the capture cost.
texts.text: There is a larger effect of employing inter-cooling than precooling on the reboiler-duty, while there is an opposite trend regarding the capture cost.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Both the combined effect of inter-cooling and pre-cooling and the effect of inter-cooling alone are larger for piperazine than MEA.
texts.text: Both the combined effect of inter-cooling and pre-cooling and the effect of inter-cooling alone are larger for piperazine than MEA.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · As for MEA the lowest reboiler duty is obtained for the combined employment of inter-cooling and pre-cooling with the lowest pre-cooling temperature. However, there seems to be a minimum capture-cost with pre-cooling closer to 40 ◦ C. Due to possible precipitation of piperazine, this higher precooling temperature is recommended anyway.
texts.text: As for MEA the lowest reboiler duty is obtained for the combined employment of inter-cooling and pre-cooling with the lowest pre-cooling temperature. However, there seems to be a minimum capture-cost with pre-cooling closer to 40 ◦ C. Due to possible precipitation of piperazine, this higher precooling temperature is recommended anyway.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Since there are uncertainties in the methodology applied here, it is chosen to summarize the main results based on the relative reduction compared to the base cases in reboiler-duty and capture cost ( D /ton of captured CO2) for the cases with lowest capture cost for each of the main cases.
texts.text: Since there are uncertainties in the methodology applied here, it is chosen to summarize the main results based on the relative reduction compared to the base cases in reboiler-duty and capture cost ( D /ton of captured CO2) for the cases with lowest capture cost for each of the main cases.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Even though there is an effect of inter-cooling with respect to reduced reboiler duty for all lignite coal and bituminous
texts.text: Even though there is an effect of inter-cooling with respect to reduced reboiler duty for all lignite coal and bituminous

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Table 6 -Relative reduction in capture cost and reboiler duty for the most optimal case (the one with lowest capture cost).
texts.text: Table 6 -Relative reduction in capture cost and reboiler duty for the most optimal case (the one with lowest capture cost).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: coal cases using MEA as solvent, the combined positive effect of pre-cooling and inter-cooling is much lower for the capture cost. Since also the side-stream from the absorber will definitely influence the complexity of the plant the combined means of flue gas cooling is not recommended. The reduction in capture cost given in Table 6 will thus decrease with approximately 2%-points. However, for the piperazine cases studied here, the combined effect is more pronounced and thus any employment of combined flue-gas cooling is highly recommendable.
texts.text: coal cases using MEA as solvent, the combined positive effect of pre-cooling and inter-cooling is much lower for the capture cost. Since also the side-stream from the absorber will definitely influence the complexity of the plant the combined means of flue gas cooling is not recommended. The reduction in capture cost given in Table 6 will thus decrease with approximately 2%-points. However, for the piperazine cases studied here, the combined effect is more pronounced and thus any employment of combined flue-gas cooling is highly recommendable.
